name: Anduril Industries
search: anduril industries anduril is a venture-backed defense product company founded in 2017, headquartered in costa mesa, ca, that builds autonomous hardware (counter-uas towers and interceptors, autonomous aircraft and undersea vehicles, rocket motors) tied together by its lattice command-and-control software [s26]. it self-funds development with private capital and sells finished products at firm-fixed prices instead of performing cost-type development work [s28]. it reported roughly $2.2b revenue in 2025, projects $4.3b for 2026, employs about 7,000 people, and raised a $5b series h in may 2026 at a $61b valuation [s1][s2][s30]. it grows by acquisition (area-i 2021, dive technologies 2022, adranos 2023, blue force technologies 2023, numerica's radar/c2 lines 2025) [s26][s27]. we do not usually meet them head-to-head on a services rfp. the collision is structural: their enterprise agreements and "systems integration partner" roles absorb integration, test-support, fielding, and sustainment-engineering scope that agencies used to buy from services firms like us, at socom (c-uas and mission autonomy sip) [s7][s9], the army (a $20b, 10-year enterprise agreement) [s3][s4], and the usaf test enterprise, where their cca is now flown and turned by blue-suit crews at edwards under the eglin-based experimental operations unit [s12][s13]. they are also building a huntsville presence aimed at the missile, c-uas, and air-and-missile-defense community on our home turf [s16]. anduril rarely beats us in a source selection; it removes the source selection. when a customer signs an enterprise agreement or names them integration partner, the integration labs, test support, fielding, and software-sustainment tasks we would have bid become vendor-delivered product scope [s3][s4][s7]. watch three lanes: (1) army/redstone, where the $20b enterprise agreement consolidates 120+ procurement actions and their huntsville office (led by retired ltg neil thurgood) targets c-uas and air-and-missile-defense work [s4][s16]; (2) socom, where sip roles for counter-uas and mission autonomy put them in the integrator seat we sell into [s7][s9]; (3) the usaf test enterprise, where their cca test narrative ("maintainers run it from a ruggedized laptop") argues for smaller government test and ops-support footprints [s12]. they also outbid everyone for autonomy and software talent, including in huntsville [s16]. - speed and willingness to build first: hardware developed on their own money and offered as a working product, not a proposal, which buys credibility with impatient customers [s28]. - lattice software as the connective layer: once it is the c2 backbone (jiatf-401 named it the preferred tactical counter-drone c2) every added sensor or effector deepens lock-in [s6]. - capital depth: $5b series h and roughly $11.9b raised lets them absorb losses that would kill a services firm's balance sheet [s1][s30]. - appetite for firm-fixed-price risk at scales primes now avoid; all task orders under the army enterprise agreement are ffp [s5]. - political and narrative top-cover as the flagship "new defense industrial base" company; golden dome software role with palantir extends that story [s22]. - senior-leader hires who open doors: retired ltg neil thurgood (former army rccto) runs their huntsville site and portfolio [s16]. - marquee enterprise positions: army 10-year, up-to-$20b enterprise agreement (mar 2026) consolidating 120+ procurement actions [s3][s4]; socom counter-uas sip idiq (~$967.6m, jan 2022; ceiling raised to ~$1.9b in nov 2024) [s7][s8]; socom mission autonomy sip ($86m, mar 2025) [s9]. - production wins in usaf autonomy: yfq-44a cca first flew oct 2025, entered serial production mar 2026, and was ordered into production alongside ga's fq-42 in jun 2026 (150 combined aircraft planned by 2030) [s10][s11]. - embedded in the usaf test enterprise: the 53rd wing's experimental operations unit flew multi-sortie cca tests at edwards in apr 2026 with front-line maintainers launching and recovering the jet [s12][s13]. - soldier program footholds: took over microsoft's ivas contract by novation (apr 2025) and won a $159m sbmc prototype award (sep 2025), teamed with meta on the eagleeye headset [s14][s15][s29]. - large fielded base at dhs/cbp: autonomous surveillance towers under an idiq with a $2b ceiling, ~$818m obligated as of dec 2025 [s21]. - international proof: first ghost shark xl-auv delivered to the royal australian navy (nov 2025) under an a$1.7b production program [s23]. - financial runway and growth: $2.2b (2025) to projected $4.3b (2026) revenue, $61b valuation [s1][s2]. - growing weapons/propulsion play: adranos acquisition (2023) and a mississippi solid-rocket-motor complex aimed at the srm shortage [s18][s26]. ### w1 — execution strain and quality escapes under hypergrowth a march 2026 wired investigation, based on interviews with current and former employees, described safety lapses, production delays, management churn, and deadline pressure across manufacturing sites; it reported the srm line missed its july 2025 mass-production target by more than a year, that the head of production was let go abruptly, and that in at least four military tests in 2025 anduril systems (including two altius drones) failed to perform as expected [s18]. in late june 2026 a rocket motor exploded during a test at their mississippi site, damaging the test stand (no injuries reported) [s19]. ### w2 — deep operating losses carried by venture capital the company projects an operating loss of roughly $1.2b for 2026 while funding simultaneous factory build-outs, with adjusted ebitda profitability not expected until about 2030 [s1]. every task order under the army enterprise agreement is firm-fixed-price, so overruns land on their books, a structure that has burned far larger primes [s5]. long-horizon support commitments depend on continued access to private capital markets. ### w3 — integrator roles that conflict with their product line as socom's counter-uas sip they are chartered to integrate "best-of-breed" third-party sensors and effectors while simultaneously selling their own competing towers, interceptors, and c2 software [s7][s8]. on ivas they took over sustainment of another vendor's hardware while positioning their own eagleeye headset for the follow-on sbmc competition [s14][s15]. our read: customers and competitors will increasingly raise organizational-conflict-of-interest and vendor-lock objections against vendor-owned integration. ### w4 — uneven reliability record on fielded, long-duration systems eff reporting on cbp's tower program, citing 2024 border patrol memos, said about 30% of the towers were broken and that the sentry towers have persistent performance problems in desert conditions [s20]. the fielded-fleet availability story is far weaker than the demo story. ### w5 — consolidation model draws scrutiny, and they litigate press coverage of the $20b army enterprise agreement flags concentration and fixed-price risk as open questions for a deal of this size [s5]. they are also willing to fight procurements: anduril protested an air force abms tactical-edge-node task order at gao (b-419420); the protest was denied and partly dismissed [s17]. judgment: single-vendor enterprise deals are politically fragile and give incumbents and congress a target. - sell independence. they cannot objectively grade their own homework (w3). position astrion as the vendor-neutral engineering and t&e arm that makes the government a smart buyer of exactly this kind of product: independent test planning, acceptance criteria, integration verification, and fleet-availability measurement. their growth makes our referee role more necessary, not less. - own the government side of the table. on cca, c-uas, and enterprise-software fielding, somebody must define requirements, run government test, and hold ffp vendors accountable for w1/w4-type escapes. that is seta, t&e, and mission-engineering scope only a non-conflicted firm can hold. - exploit the sustainment gap. their dna is product launch, not decades of on-range, on-site support (w1, w4). emphasize our staffed, badged, enduring presence at aftc, redstone, and customer sites, and our record of keeping government test infrastructure running. - make availability data the battleground. push customers to demand fielded-system readiness metrics and government-verified test results before enterprise commitments (w4). we can build and run those scorecards. - team where it pays. relationship may become "both": their products need range time, instrumentation, test conduct, safety packages, and v&v we already deliver inside the fence. pursue sub/associate roles on their test campaigns rather than ceding the whole lane. (needs internal input: any existing teaming contacts or ndas with them) - defend the talent base. expect huntsville and edwards/eglin poaching at product-company compensation (per their huntsville expansion [s16]). (needs internal input: attrition data and counter-offer posture for autonomy/software staff) - **they say:** "they deliver working hardware on their own dime; you deliver briefings and labor hours." **we say:** "self-funded hardware still has to pass somebody's independent test, integrate with everything the vendor doesn't sell, and stay mission-capable for years. that verification and integration work is what we do, and it is exactly what protects you when a fixed-price vendor misses (their own fielded-fleet record shows why it matters)." - **they say:** "the enterprise agreement already covers integration and support; we don't need a services contract." **we say:** "an enterprise agreement covers the vendor's products on the vendor's terms. ask who verifies performance claims, who integrates the systems they compete against, and who represents the government's technical position at the table. that cannot be the same company cashing the product check." - **they say:** "their cca test showed airmen operating it with just a laptop; test support footprints are shrinking." **we say:** "lean operations at the flight line is real progress, and it shifts the work rather than deleting it: autonomy verification, test design, range integration, safety cases, and data analysis grow as autonomy scales. we should size the government test enterprise for that, not assume it away." - **they say:** "dod policy favors buying commercial products over paying for development services." **we say:** "agreed, and buying commercial well is a skill. smart-buyer engineering, acceptance testing, and oci-clean integration are how you get the benefits of commercial speed without inheriting the vendor's risk." - "when the integrator also manufactures competing sensors and effectors, who independently verifies the trade studies and the performance claims?" - "can you get government-verified fleet availability data for their currently fielded systems over the last 24 months, not demo results?" - "if every task order is firm-fixed-price and the vendor is running a multi-billion-dollar annual operating loss, what protects your program's support tail five years from now?" - "what is the exit plan and data-rights position if you need to swap the c2 layer or add a sensor the vendor competes with?" - "how will the government retain an independent test and acceptance capability as vendor-embedded teams take over more of the fielding pipeline?" ### g1 — vendor-neutral integration - ghost: "offerors should describe how they will provide vendor-neutral integration and objective evaluation of candidate sensors and effectors, including specific mitigations for organizational conflicts of interest where an integrator also manufactures competing products." - targets: w3 - proof: pp3 ### g2 — fielded availability, not demo performance - ghost: "offerors should substantiate sustained operational availability of previously fielded systems with government-verifiable readiness data covering at least twenty-four months of field operations, as distinct from demonstration or exercise results." - veteran of more than 30 years in the u.s. army, who remains chairman [s1][s31]. third-party trackers put it at roughly 1,100-1,500 employees and $250m-$360m revenue (est.), with capability lines in systems and software engineering, cyber and information warfare, data science, uncrewed systems, and federal support/enterprise it services [s2][s3]. its center of gravity is redstone: an amcom 2023), subs to caci on nasa's $2b ncaps it contract, supports space force space electronic warfare through the bounty hunter program it has served since 2011, and keeps an eglin foothold dating to the tams/tmas era [s22][s23][s24][s11][s13]. we collide across our anchor accounts: redstone a&as task orders (express, s3i, smdc, mda), the usaf test enterprise (where it currently subs to us on the name: Credence Management Solutions
search: credence management solutions credence credence management solutions llc credence dynamo solutions credence management solutions is a founder-owned federal services firm headquartered in vienna, va, started in 2005 by sid chowdhary (ceo) and run since late 2011 with president prashant gaur as a two-principal shop; it has grown entirely organically, with no acquisitions or outside capital in public records [s1][s2][s3][s4]. scale today: $500m+ claimed revenue, #61 on the 2025 washington technology top 100, roughly 1,200 to 1,800 staff (est.) after the 2025 usaid layoffs [s4][s5][s6][s23]. the business has three lanes: department of the air force acquisition and engineering support (a deep epass task-order portfolio across aflcmc plus space force staff support), global health and international development (the usaid franchise, gutted in 2025), and federal civilian and defense it (dla, nih, state) [s9][s10][s11][s13][s24][s25][s26]. their offices sit on top of aflcmc's field operating locations: fairborn oh at wright-patterson, warner robins ga at robins, and fort walton beach fl in the eglin community, plus vienna and dc [s7]. we collide with them in the aflcmc a&as ecosystem where our legacy oasis systems business is an anchor incumbent, in daf digital and cyber staff support, and now at space systems command, where they took a $200m+ command staff support order and held it through protests [s9][s11][s13][s19]. the relationship is not purely adversarial: at eglin they are listed beside astrion as teaming partners on odyssey's eb (armament directorate) epass task [s33]. they claim to prime 95 percent of their work and were the self-described top performer on oasis sb pools 1 and 3 with roughly 50 oasis prime task orders [s8]. with their biggest single franchise (usaid) destroyed in 2025, their growth engine has one obvious place to point: defense services, which means at us. credence wins staff-heavy acquisition-support competitions on price, speed, and vehicle position, inside aflcmc and now at space force. they hold a decade-deep epass portfolio (their tenth epass task order came in may 2022, and the 2023 "franken-cutter" award consolidated two programs plus crows support) [s9][s10], and they reach set-aside work we cannot touch through an 8(a) mentor-protege jv that took the $337.6m c2isr epass order on oasis sb in october 2024 [s11][s12]. at ssc they beat small-business a&as peers for a $200-222m command staff support order and survived two gao protests, one of which forced a re-evaluation of whether their professional compensation was priced too low; that is the clearest public signal of how they compete on rates [s13][s14][s15][s16]. oasis+ unrestricted across six domains, including enterprise solutions (they say one of only 29 awardees), gives them a seat at the big consolidated orders we prime or chase [s18]. what they do not have: hands-on test and evaluation, range, aviation and missile engineering, or any documented footprint at aftc, redstone, mda, navair/navsea, or nasa. expect them wherever an evaluation reduces to staffing plans and price; do not expect them (yet) where facilities, instrumentation, and deep technical past performance decide. - rate competitiveness in professional-services evaluations: lean, founder-owned cost structure with no outside investors to feed, and a documented willingness to price professional compensation at levels competitors formally challenge [s14] (analysis; see pricing posture). - continuity story inside aflcmc: ten-plus epass task orders across hq afmc, c2isr, crows, and other directorates, with offices at all three main aflcmc hubs, so evaluators see a known quantity [s7][s9][s10][s11]. - a vehicle-and-jv lattice that covers every on-ramp: legacy oasis sb and unrestricted, oasis+ unrestricted (six domains), astro, ites-3s, gsa mas, dla jets 2.0, plus an 8(a) mentor-protege jv to keep set-aside channels open after size graduation [s8][s11][s12][s18][s26][s34]. - founder-led speed: two principals since 2011 make teaming, pricing, and bid decisions without a board or private-equity approval chain [s3][s4] (judgment). - workforce brand that recruits: washington post #1 government technology services top workplace and #2 overall for 2025, inc. 5000 honoree [s36]. - demonstrated bounce-back capacity: within months of the usaid collapse they booked a $107m state department contract and a $118.6m dla task order against 18 bidders [s25][s26]. - epass franchise depth: tenth epass task order by may 2022 (hq afmc a&as at wright-patterson); the december 2023 franken-cutter award merged two of their epass programs and added crows support, serving 17+ organizations with acquisition, engineering, scientific, financial, logistics, test, intelligence, and security-management staff [s9][s10]. - $337,639,354 c2isr division epass task order (fa872124fb005, october 2024) via credence dynamo solutions on oasis sb, supporting aflcmc's peo digital portfolio c2isr division work at robins afb [s11][s12]. - space force beachhead: $200-222m ssc advanced systems and development directorate command staff support order (directorate hq at kirtland afb with divisions at la afb, hill afb, and jsc), defended through btas and quantech gao protests and a corrective-action re-evaluation [s13][s14][s15][s16][s17]. - oasis+ unrestricted position (47qrca25du090, awarded december 17, 2024): six domains including enterprise solutions, management & advisory, technical & engineering, r&d, intelligence services, and logistics; first documented order is the $23.8m daf zero trust functional management office support task (august 2025) [s18][s19]. - vehicle breadth beyond oasis+: legacy oasis sb pools 1 and 3 (self-described top performer, ~50 oasis prime task orders, 95 percent prime), legacy oasis unrestricted, gsa astro, army ites-3s (unrestricted and sb), multiple gsa mas contracts, dla jets 2.0 [s8][s26][s34]. - jv production line: credence dynamo solutions has taken a $74m army resource-management order, a $45.8m army pmose task on oasis sb, isr services support at shaw afb ($28.6m cumulative through option year two), and a prime geoint support contract [s12][s29][s30][s37]. - ai/ml toehold at dla: defense automatic addressing system ai/ml task plus an ai/ml services contract [s27]; they also advertise advanced battle management system support [s35]. - growth and recognition: $500m+ claimed revenue as one of the few fully organically grown wt top 100 firms, washington post top workplaces #1 in government technology services for 2025, inc. 5000 honoree [s4][s5][s36]. - protest-hardened: their two biggest known franchises (ghtasc and ssc css) both survived multi-round gao challenges [s13][s14][s21]. ### w1 — their biggest franchise was destroyed in 2025 and the replacement book is a fraction of it credence held an $800m usaid global health task order (ghtasc, won 2022) and a ~$899m usaid bureau for humanitarian assistance support task order (2024) [s20][s21][s22]. the january 2025 stop-work orders hit immediately: npr reported credence, one of three main institutional-support staffing contractors to usaid, laid off nearly 400 employees within days [s23]. usaid formally ceased operations july 1, 2025, with roughly 83 percent of its programs canceled and the remainder moved to state [s24]. documented replacement wins since (a $107m state refugee-processing contract, a $118.6m dla task order) total well under the ceiling value of what collapsed [s25][s26]. that lost revenue creates pressure to win defense work fast, and pressure like that produces buy-in pricing and overreach (judgment). ### w2 — the portfolio is advisory staff support, not hands-on technical delivery every major dod win in public view is a&as or staff support: epass acquisition, financial, logistics, and security-management support to aflcmc offices [s9][s10][s11], command staff support at ssc [s13][s16], resource management and program-management support for the army [s29]. public sources reviewed for this card show no prime work at aftc, no redstone (avmc/amcom/smdc) presence, nothing at mda, navair, navsea, or nasa, and no operated labs, ranges, test facilities, or fielded hardware story (needs internal input: fpds pull to confirm the absence before treating it as settled). where an evaluation weighs test conduct, instrumentation, or engineering execution rather than staffing plans, they have little to cite. ### w3 — growth scaffolding rides small-business channels they have outgrown the marquee air force wins ride oasis sb, either directly from their legacy pools or through the credence dynamo 8(a) mentor-protege jv [s8][s11][s12]. as a graduated large business, their direct small-business channel dies with the legacy oasis sb ordering window, sba joint-venture rules limit how much of a set-aside job the mentor can perform, and the protege relationship has a regulatory shelf life. their unrestricted successor position (oasis+ u, december 2024) is real but young: one documented order as of this writing [s18][s19]. the transition years are the window to beat them in full-and-open competition before their unrestricted past performance accumulates (judgment on timing; facts cited). ### w4 — professional compensation drew formal realism scrutiny, and they cut staff fast under stress in the ssc css protests, gao's outcome-prediction adr concluded the agency had not properly evaluated credence's professional compensation plan under far 52.222-46 (the provision aimed at lowballed professional salaries on recompetes); gsa re-evaluated in corrective action and reaffirmed the award, and the final protest was denied [s14]. the award stood, but the record documents that their compensation levels were contestable ground. separately, the january 2025 stop-work response was near-immediate layoffs of ~400 people [s23]. for incumbent workforces deciding whose offer letter to sign at transition, both facts are usable (judgment on the use; facts cited). ### w5 — no presence in our anchor technical communities their listed offices are vienna, washington dc, fairborn, warner robins, and fort walton beach [s7]. nothing in huntsville, at edwards, in the hanscom/lexington cluster, at pax river, or in albuquerque appears in public listings, and their documented eglin-community role is as one of eight subcontractor partners on odyssey's armament directorate epass team, not a prime seat [s7][s33]. recruiting and surging cleared technical staff in those communities without a local bench is slow and expensive (judgment). - make evaluations technical (w2). push customers to score hands-on engineering execution: test conduct, instrumentation, lab and range operation, integration work, prime technical past performance in the mission area. their epass-style citations are administrative and program-office support; ours are not. this is the single sharpest separator. - time captures to their vehicle transition (w3). legacy oasis sb orders and the 8(a) jv channel are wasting assets. in full-and-open recompetes on oasis+ or agency idiqs, they show up with thin unrestricted past performance; force citations to be prime, recent, and comparable in contract type and size. - force jv transparency (w3). where they bid through a mentor-protege jv, drive questions about managing-venturer workshare, sba performance-of-work compliance, and which entity's people and processes actually execute. - contest staffing economics (w4). ask for total compensation plans evaluated per far 52.222-46, comparison to incumbent salary levels, and program-level retention data. pair our named key personnel and retention record against their protest-documented compensation scrutiny (needs internal input: our retention figures by program). - sell stability against their 2025 shock (w1). corporate steadiness, backlog quality, and staff continuity through funding turbulence are now legitimate discriminators; ask evaluators to weigh them. - out-local them (w5). in huntsville, at edwards, at eglin's test wing, and in the hanscom community, our badge-in-hand density and community tenure beat a proposal-time hiring plan. make on-site presence a scored item. - manage the teammate line at eglin. we sit on the same odyssey eb team [s33]; assume they see part of our staffing and rate behavior there, and be deliberate about what they can observe. where they grow ambitions from sub to prime in armament-adjacent scopes, treat them as a named threat early (needs internal input: eb workshare boundaries). (anticipated pushback, team judgment; replace with actual field feedback as it accumulates.) - **they say:** "they'll do it cheaper." **we say:** ask what the labor mix and compensation plan look like at that price. in their highest-profile recent win, the government had to re-run the cost evaluation of their professional compensation under far 52.222-46 before the award stuck. underpaying professionals on a technical services contract is how incumbent capture fails and quality drains out; ask every offeror for comp-to-incumbent comparisons and retention history. - **they say:** "they're everywhere at aflcmc; the customer knows them." **we say:** knowing the program office is not the same as doing the engineering. their portfolio is advisory and administrative support; ask offerors to cite prime-executed technical work, with named facilities, instrumentation, and test products, of this scope. - **they say:** "they're a top workplace; they'll staff it fast." **we say:** staffing speed matters less than staying power. when their largest customer issued stop-work orders, roughly 400 staff were laid off within days. ask for continuity commitments and program retention rates, not recruiting awards. - **they say:** "they rebuilt after usaid; they're financially fine." **we say:** their documented replacement wins are a small fraction of the ceilings they lost, which is exactly when a firm gets aggressive on price to rebuild volume. buy-in pricing is a performance risk the evaluation can and should test. - name: General Dynamics Mission Systems
search: general dynamics mission systems gdms general dynamics c4 systems general dynamics advanced information systems general dynamics mission systems (gdms) is the c5isr products-and-integration arm of general dynamics, formed in january 2015 by combining gd c4 systems and gd advanced information systems [s2]. it sits with gdit inside gd's technologies segment, which booked $13.47b revenue and $1.28b operating earnings (9.5% margin) in fy2025 with roughly 40,000 employees across the two units [s3][s31]. gdms itself is roughly 12,000 people across 80+ facilities (est.), headquartered in the northern virginia area [s28]. its franchise positions are secure communications and crypto (taclane, ngld-m) [s1][s11], navy submarine electronics and lifecycle support (including the progeny systems business) [s21], strategic-deterrence weapon c2 [s27], and space ground segments (muos, sda's proliferated warfighter space architecture) [s12][s6]. we do not collide with them on hardware. we collide on the services and integration adjacencies wrapped around major programs: sda/ssc ground operations and sustainment, navy combat-system engineering and software support, and army c5isr integration, where gdms sells engineering services as pull-through around its own product baselines and its gdit sibling primes the pure-services work [s7][s21][s15]. gdms hurts us where an engineering-services scope sits next to hardware they already own. on muos ground sustainment they are the sole-source incumbent with up to $2.2b of runway [s12][s13][s14]; on sda ground they hold both the operations-and-integration and ground-management franchises [s6][s7]; on submarines progeny gives them the software and lifecycle-services lane inside navsea programs [s21]. when a customer bundles integration services with the fielded baseline, we start from behind. the compounding threat is the gd family play: gdms takes the product-adjacent work while gdit primes the large consolidated services contracts (for example the $988m navy sacss c5isr support award) [s15], squeezing mid-size primes from both sides. they rarely meet us head-on in seta, t&e, or range support; the fight is on systems-integration and o&m task orders at ssc/sda, navsea, and army c5isr programs. - ownership of fielded baselines: the customer's crypto devices, satcom ground system, or combat-system components are often gdms products, which makes them the default choice for the surrounding engineering and sustainment work [s11][s12][s21]. - nsa-certified crypto pedigree and classified infrastructure: type 1 encryption franchise (ngld-m: 265,000 units planned over 10 years) that few companies can touch [s11]. - proliferated-constellation ground expertise at sda: they designed and operate the tranche 1 ops centers and won the follow-on ground management and integration design contract [s7][s6]. - corporate reach-back and balance sheet of a $52.6b prime [s4], plus a services sibling (gdit) that lets the gd family bid both product and services sides of a mission area [s3][s15]. - seven decades of strategic-deterrence weapon c2 heritage that anchors credibility with nuclear-enterprise customers [s27]. - incumbent sole-source position on muos ground sustainment (10-year idiq awarded 2019 at $731.8m; max potential value $2.2b per gd's 10-k; fresh $106m ssc task order with options to $294.9m through may 2031) [s12][s13][s14]. - anchor sda ground franchises: $324.5m tranche 1 operations & integration (2022, teamed with iridium, ops centers at grand forks afb) and $491.6m ground management and integration design and development (sept 2024) [s7][s8][s6]. - growing again after a flat stretch: mission systems revenue up 11.7% in q1 2026, driven by space and international c5isr volume; technologies backlog $17.7b, book-to-bill 1.3x [s5]. - deep navy submarine services bench via progeny systems: $121m submarine software development, $66m darts, $15.3m security-toolkit award (to $91m with options), and an $18.2m aukus weapon-launch/simulation award [s21][s20][s23][s22]. - open-architecture positioning on army c5isr: won the initial cmff (cmoss mounted form factor) prototype award, $28.3m over two years via the c5 consortium ota [s9][s10]. - stable, connected leadership: president chris brady since january 2019, a six-time wash100 honoree, with recent senior hires in strategy, it, and space [s17][s18][s19]. ### w1 — product-oem posture limits objectivity on services work gdms is fundamentally a product and platform-integration house: crypto devices, satcom ground systems, combat-system hardware and software baselines [s11][s12][s21]. on any scope that involves independent assessment, test, or government-side advisory work touching those baselines, they carry an organizational-conflict-of-interest burden a non-oem services prime does not. their muos sustainment position is sole-source around their own system [s12], which customers increasingly flag as lock-in risk when they push for open architectures and data rights. ### w2 — multi-year revenue stagnation during legacy-program transition gd reported a slight revenue decline at mission systems in 2024, attributing it to the transition "from legacy work to new growth lines of business"; segment growth that year was carried by gdit's fourth consecutive year of growth, with technologies revenue up 1.6% to $13.1b [s25]. gd's fy2025 10-k notes gdit grew faster than mission systems, diluting segment margin [s3]. the unit only returned to clear growth in q1 2026 [s5]. capture teams can expect them to defend legacy revenue aggressively while their growth bets (space, cmff) are still converting. ### w3 — second priority inside gd technologies for services captures the big consolidated services wins in their own back yard go to sibling gdit, not gdms: the $988m navy sacss c5isr support contract is a gdit award [s15], and gd's 10-k describes the two units going to market together "when appropriate" [s3]. on a pure engineering-services evaluation, gdms's own past performance is product-flavored; they either lean on gdit cpars (inviting relevance challenges) or bid work that is not their core. ### w4 — history of losing software-defined competitions to more agile entrants when the army moved dcgs-a to a commercial-software model, gdms lost to raytheon and palantir (march 2018) and gao denied its protest of the rejection of its proposal (b-416181) [s16]. dated, but it is the documented pattern: where an evaluation rewards commercial software agility over mil-spec integration heritage, their model has been beaten. ### w5 — thin footprint in our anchor geographies their huntsville presence is a single cummings research park office, listed in public directories at an estimated 100 to 249 staff [s26], against the thousands of local engineers the redstone customer community expects from an entrenched services partner. public materials show no comparable footprint at the usaf test-enterprise centers (needs internal input: confirm any gdms staffing at eglin, edwards, or on aftc task orders). - sell oem independence against w1. we own no hardware baseline, so our engineering, integration support, and t&e advice carries no product agenda and no oci mitigation plan the customer has to police. push evaluation language on objectivity, data rights, and open-architecture verification wherever their products sit on the program. - against their baseline incumbency (muos, sda ground, submarine electronics), do not attack the product; attack the wrap. position us for the government-side engineering, independent assessment, integration v&v, and test scopes that a prudent pmo keeps separate from the oem. - exploit w3 by forcing services evaluations onto services terms: dedicated key personnel, unit-level past performance relevance, and program-office intimacy. their strongest services cpars often belongs to a sibling unit, not the bidding entity. - exploit w5 at redstone, aftc, and mda: our people already sit with these customers. make local bench, clearance density on site, and surge-in-place a scored discriminator. - on software-centric work (w4), stress our agile delivery record and commercial tooling; frame mil-spec integration heritage as the wrong reference class for the scope. - where they are genuinely strong (space ground, crypto, submarine combat systems), consider teaming or deconfliction rather than head-on primes; the winnable ground is the services adjacency, not the baseline. - **they say:** "the oem already knows the system; picking anyone else adds risk." **we say:** oem familiarity is real, and it is exactly why the government separates independent engineering and test from the baseline owner. we give the pmo an advocate with no product revenue at stake, and we inherit system knowledge through government-furnished artifacts and data rights the customer already paid for. - **they say:** "they bring 40,000-person corporate reach-back." **we say:** reach-back that is shared across an entire segment is a staffing promise, not a staffing plan. ask every offeror what percent of named key personnel are dedicated full time to this contract and sitting locally. that is a question we win. - **they say:** "their past performance is bigger." **we say:** bigger is not more relevant. their headline contracts are product and ground-system builds; ours are services of this scope, at this customer, at this contract size. cpars relevance criteria, applied honestly, favor us. - **they say:** "they won the open-architecture prototype, so they own the roadmap." **we say:** a prototype ota is not a production franchise, and open standards exist precisely so the government is never dependent on one vendor. we help the customer keep it that way. - "how will you ensure offerors with fielded hardware or software baselines on this program identify and mitigate organizational conflicts of interest, so the government keeps independent engineering advice?" - "should the evaluation require that cited past performance was executed by the specific business unit proposing, rather than an affiliated company?" - "what percentage of proposed key personnel will be dedicated full time and resident within commuting distance of the government site on day one?" - "how will offerors demonstrate that integration artifacts and interface documentation will be delivered with government purpose rights, so follow-on work stays competitive?" - "can offerors show recent agile software delivery on programs of this type, as opposed to hardware-baseline integration heritage?" ### g1 — oem independence and oci - ghost: "offerors should identify any organizational test advice to the government." - targets: w1 - proof: pp5 ### g2 — services work led by the performing unit - ghost: "offerors should demonstrate that cited past performance was executed by the name: Torch Technologies
search: torch technologies torch torch technologies is a 100% employee-owned engineering-services company founded in huntsville in october 2002 by bill roark and don holder; it sits under esop holding company starfish holdings alongside freedom real estate & capital and simvana, and reorganized as a public benefit corporation in 2023 [s1][s2][s24][s29]. its core business is modeling & simulation, hardware-in-the-loop (hwil) test, weapons analysis, systems engineering, and rdt&e advisory support, with self-declared primary customers of army amcom and the missile defense agency [s1][s2]. the franchise position is the devcom avmc s3i directorate at redstone, where in may 2021 it took three oasis task orders worth nearly $2.9b: $1.065b hwil systems-of-systems, $1.1b hwil missile, and $722m m&s for aviation and missile systems [s3][s4][s5][s6]. it has opened a second front in our usaf test-enterprise home turf, holding the $474.9m tmas2 tenants task order for the 96th test wing at eglin and a $186m tmas armament task order spanning eglin and kirtland [s7][s8]. headcount is roughly 1,300 to 1,500 (est.), with washington technology ranking it #60 on the top 100 [s26][s34]; offices include huntsville, shalimar (fl), albuquerque, colorado springs, aberdeen, boston, detroit, honolulu, and patuxent river, with employee-owners at kwajalein and in egypt [s1][s2]. this is our most direct size-and-lane peer: same town, same customers (avmc/amcom, mda, smdc, and now aftc), same mid-size prime strategy, same labor pool. torch hurts us most where redstone m&s and hwil incumbency r&d contract for acquisition-lifecycle capability evaluation (january 2026) [s3][s4][s10]. at mda they ride the teams/teams-next family as prime and sub, so we meet them across missile-defense m&s,
